title: Formal Hindi & Official Registers
learningGoal: Transition from casual, English-heavy street Hindi to the highly formal, Sanskritized Hindi (Shuddh Hindi) used in news, government, literature, and formal ceremonies.
instructions: In this block, we strip away the English loan words and Urdu slang you use every day, replacing them with 'Shuddh Hindi' (Pure Hindi). You will also learn the Subjunctive mood, which is used for extremely polite requests and suggestions.
conceptTitle: Shuddh Hindi and The Subjunctive Mood

lessonContent.bodyText:
Everyday spoken Hindi is heavily mixed with English and Urdu (often called Hindustani). For example, most people say 'Time kya hai?' or 'Mujhe help chahiye'.

However, in official letters, news broadcasts, government offices, and formal ceremonies (like weddings), 'Shuddh Hindi' is used. This register relies almost exclusively on Sanskrit-derived vocabulary.

Compare:
- Casual: Time kya hai? (What is the time?)
- Formal: Samay kya hai? (समय क्या है?)

- Casual: Mujhe help chahiye. (I need help.)
- Formal: Mujhe sahayata chahiye. (मुझे सहायता चाहिए।)

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: This is very important information. (Important -> Mahatvapoorna)

lessonContent.bodyText:
To speak formally, you must swap your casual vocabulary for Sanskrit-based words:

- Problem (Urdu: Pareshani) -> Samasya (समस्या)
- Question (Urdu: Sawaal) -> Prashna (प्रश्न)
- Answer (Urdu: Jawaab) -> Uttar (उत्तर)
- Wait (Urdu: Intezaar) -> Pratiksha (प्रतीक्षा)
- Begin (Urdu: Shuru) -> Prarambh (प्रारंभ)
- End (Urdu: Khatam) -> Samapt (समाप्त)
- Request (Urdu: Guzarish) -> Anurodh (अनुरोध)
- Permission (Urdu: Ijazat) -> Anumati (अनुमति)

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I have a request of you.

lessonContent.bodyText:
The Subjunctive mood is used to express wishes, permissions, suggestions, or uncertainty. It sounds like the Future tense, but without the 'ga/gi/ge' at the end.

Formation: Take the Future tense (e.g., Jaunga, Karega) and chop off the 'ga'.
- Main jaunga (I will go) -> Main jaun (May I go / I should go).
- Hum karenge (We will do) -> Hum karein (Shall we do / We should do).

If you want to ask for permission politely, NEVER use 'Can I go?' (Kya main jaa sakta hoon?). Instead, use the Subjunctive: 'Kya main jaun?' (May I go / Shall I go?).

In casual Hindi, to say 'You should study', you say 'Tumhein padhna chahiye'. 

In highly formal Hindi, you split the sentence: 'It is needed THAT you study'. 
- Aapko chahiye KI aap padhein.

Notice that the verb after 'ki' (that) MUST be in the subjunctive mood (padhein, not padhenge).

Example: The government should build roads.
- Formal: Sarkaar ko chahiye ki woh sadkein banaye. (सरकार को चाहिए कि वह सड़कें बनाए।)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: हमें चाहिए कि हम नियमों का पालन करें।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Humein chahiye ki hum niyamon ka paalan karein.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: We ought to follow the rules.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: छात्रों को चाहिए कि वे कड़ी मेहनत करें।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Chhatron ko chahiye ki ve kadi mehnat karein.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Students should work hard. (Lit: It is needed to students that they work hard).

lessonContent.bodyText:
To explain the purpose of an action ('I did this SO THAT that could happen'), use the conjunction 'Taaki' (ताकि).

The clause following 'Taaki' MUST also be in the Subjunctive mood, because the outcome is a wish or goal, not a guaranteed reality.

Example: I am speaking slowly so that you can understand.
- Main dheere bol raha hoon TAAKI aap samajh sakein. (मैं धीरे बोल रहा हूँ ताकि आप समझ सकें।)
(Note: sakein = may be able to, not sakenge = will be able to).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: जल्दी चलो ताकि हम ट्रेन पकड़ सकें।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Jaldi chalo taaki hum train pakad sakein.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Walk fast so that we may catch the train.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: मैंने उसे पत्र लिखा ताकि उसे सच्चाई पता चले।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Maine use patra likha taaki use sachchai pata chale.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I wrote him a letter so that he may know the truth.

lessonContent.sectionTitle: 6. Using 'Heta' (Due to / By means of) [Highly Formal]
lessonContent.bodyText:
In extremely formal letters or invitations, the word 'Ke dwar/dwara' (by) or 'Ke kaaran' (because of) is sometimes replaced by more sophisticated Sanskrit terms like 'Ke hetu' (for the purpose of) or 'Ke madhyam se' (through the medium of).

Example: For the purpose of education.
- Casual: Padhai ke liye.
- Formal: Shiksha ke hetu. (शिक्षा के हेतु)

These words elevate the tone to an administrative or literary level.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: इस पत्र के माध्यम से आपको सूचित किया जाता है...
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Is patra ke madhyam se aapko soochit kiya jaata hai...
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Through the medium of this letter, you are informed...

learningObjective: The learner will command the formal Sanskritized register of Hindi, utilizing advanced vocabulary and the subjunctive mood to issue polite requests, formal recommendations, and state purposes.

examples.targetText: कृपया मुझे अंदर आने की अनुमति दें।
examples.transliteration: Kripya mujhe andar aane ki anumati dein.
examples.baseTranslation: Please give me permission to come inside.
examples.explanation: Uses pure formal vocabulary (Kripya, Anumati) and the subjunctive 'dein' instead of the casual imperative 'do'.

examples.targetText: मैं आपसे अनुरोध करता हूँ कि आप मेरी समस्या का समाधान करें।
examples.transliteration: Main aapse anurodh karta hoon ki aap meri samasya ka samadhan karein.
examples.baseTranslation: I request you that you resolve my problem.
examples.explanation: A classic formal letter structure. 'Anurodh' (request), 'Samasya' (problem), 'Samadhan' (resolution), followed by the subjunctive 'karein'.

importantRules.ruleText: Any clause following 'Taaki' (So that) MUST be in the subjunctive mood (e.g., karein, jaaye, ho), not the future tense (karenge, jayega, hoga).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Taaki expresses intent, not a guaranteed future, hence the subjunctive.

importantRules.ruleText: When asking 'Shall I?' or 'May I?', drop the 'ga/gi/ge' from the future tense. 'Main jaunga' -> 'Main jaun?'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Using 'Sakta hoon' (Can I) sounds blunt. 'Jaun' sounds culturally refined.

cultureContext.contextText: India has a stark divide between the language of the streets and the language of the state. If you go to a police station, government office, or read a wedding invitation, you will encounter 'Shuddh Hindi'. Knowing this register commands immense respect. If a foreigner uses words like 'Pratiksha' (Wait) instead of 'Intezaar', native speakers will be stunned by their education level.
dosList: Use 'Prashna' (Question) and 'Uttar' (Answer) in academic or formal settings.
Always use the subjunctive 'Kya main jaun?' when asking elders for permission to leave.
dontsList: Do not use highly formal Sanskritized Hindi with street vendors or taxi drivers; it sounds pretentious and confusing.

summary: You have entered the highest tier of Hindi. By replacing common Urdu/English words with their Sanskrit roots, and by mastering the polite, uncertain 'Subjunctive' mood, you can now write formal letters, understand government notices, and speak with profound respect.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the formal Sanskritized word for 'Problem' (instead of the Urdu 'Pareshani')?
How do you ask 'Shall we go?' using the subjunctive mood?
Which conjunction is used to mean 'So that' (e.g., I am running SO THAT I can catch the train)?

keyTakeaways: Shuddh Hindi uses Sanskrit roots (Samasya, Prashna, Pratiksha).
Use Subjunctive for 'May I?', 'Shall we?', and after 'Taaki' (So that).
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastering the official register of Hindi and the subjunctive mood for intent/permission.
importantFacts: Official Hindi actively avoids English and Urdu loan words.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners can draft formal emails and speak politely using subjunctive verb forms.

feedbackRules.onPass: Astounding! You are ready to speak with politicians, professors, and government officials.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the Subjunctive mood (Section 3). Just take the future tense and chop off the end! Karunga -> Karun.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort. Memorize the vocabulary list in Section 2. Switching 'Sawaal/Jawaab' to 'Prashna/Uttar' instantly elevates your Hindi.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: कृपया यहाँ हस्ताक्षर करें। यह बहुत महत्वपूर्ण सूचना है। मेरा आपसे एक अनुरोध है। कार्यक्रम समाप्त हो गया है। क्या मैं अंदर आऊँ? हम अब क्या करें? हमें चाहिए कि हम नियमों का पालन करें। छात्रों को चाहिए कि वे कड़ी मेहनत करें। जल्दी चलो ताकि हम ट्रेन पकड़ सकें। मैंने उसे पत्र लिखा ताकि उसे सच्चाई पता चले। इस पत्र के माध्यम से आपको सूचित किया जाता है...

hintText: To find the subjunctive form, ask yourself: 'How do I say I WILL do this?' (Karunga). Now drop the 'ga' -> Karun.
commonMistakes: Using the future tense after 'Taaki' (e.g., 'Taaki main jaunga' instead of 'Taaki main jaun').
